title: E. coli turns plastic waste into Parkinson’s drug
html_body:
Microorganisms in health, the environment and food systems |
View this email in your browser Thursday 19 March 2026

Produced with support from:

Hello Nature readers,
This week, we discover a new species of ‘magic mushroom’, learn that an engineered strain of E. coli can turn plastic waste into a Parkinson’s drug and consider the state of play of vaccine policy in the United States.
‘Magic mushrooms’, such as the Psilocybe cubensis shown here, produce psychoactive compounds that cause hallucinations if ingested. (Wirestock/Getty)

New species rewrites the history of ‘shrooms

A newly described species of ‘magic mushroom’ could upend a popular theory of when psychedelic fungi popped up around the world. Researchers had thought that the magic mushrooms that grow in southern Africa were Psilocybe cubensis, the same species that grows in the Americas. Closer inspection revealed that the African mushrooms are a separate species, now named Psilocybe ochraceocentrata, and last shared a common ancestor with P. cubensis approximately 1.5 million years ago. These findings scupper the hypothesis that P. cubensis was inadvertently introduced to the Americas by 16th-century settlers, but the research offers no clues as to its origins across the Atlantic.

Popular Science | 6 min read
 Reference: Proceedings of the Royal Society B paper

Antibiotics leave their mark on microbiomes

A single course of antibiotics can have a years-long impact on make-up of a person’s gut microbiome. By comparing the prescription records and stool samples of nearly 15,000 people, researchers found that people who had taken commonly used classes of antibiotics called tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones had reduced microbiome diversity that persisted for four to eight years. People who had taken the drug clindamycin also had on average 17 fewer bacterial species in their gut than people who hadn’t taken the drug.

The Microbiologist | 5 min read
 Reference: Nature Medicine paper

E. coli turns plastic into Parkinson’s drug

Researchers have used an engineered strain of Escherichia coli to turn polyethylene terephthalate (PET) plastic waste into levodopa, a medication used to treat Parkinson’s disease. The team say this process produces levodopa at high yields, and they used the alga Chlamydomonas reinhardtii to capture some of the carbon dioxide produced by the reaction. Before E. coli can get to work, PET must be broken down into blocks of terephthalic acid in a separate process. But this technique is still more sustainable than conventional methods of levodopa synthesis, which rely on fossil fuels, the team says.

The Microbiologist | 6 min read
 Reference: Nature Sustainability paper

Gut microbe fuels memory loss in mice

A species of gut bacteria that proliferates as mice get older plays a part in cognitive decline. Researchers found that the bacterium, Parabacteroides goldsteinii, interferes with signalling along sensory nerves that connect the gut to the brain by producing fatty acids, which trigger the release of inflammatory molecules from immune cells. Although the experiments were conducted in mice, the gut–brain circuit that the team identified “is likely conserved in humans”, says biochemist David Vauzour,. If confirmed, the link could help to develop gut-targeted therapies to reverse cognitive decline in people.

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: Nature paper

US vaccine chaos

Court halts RFK Jr’s vaccine plans

A US judge has blocked sweeping changes to the country’s childhood immunization schedule brought in by health secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr. The case was brought by the American Academy of Pediatrics, the American College of Physicians and the Infectious Diseases Society of America, among others. The judge called Kennedy to task for replacing the members of a prominent vaccine-advisory panel with people who largely lacked expertise in vaccines, and reversed all of the panel’s decisions. The ruling opens with a quote from astronomer Carl Sagan: “‘Science,’ like law, ‘is far from a perfect instrument of knowledge’,” it says. “Nevertheless, science is still ‘the best we have’.”

The New York Times | 8 min read
 Reference: US court ruling

Vaccines divide MAGA and MAHA

With elections for Congress imminent in the United States, the administration of President Donald Trump is pulling back from vaccine reform policies and focusing on issues that are more popular with key voters, administration officials say. “We’re just kind of done with the vaccine issue,” said one, who spoke to STAT under the condition of anonymity. The stance change has proven unpopular among the Make America Healthy Again (MAHA) movement, led by Robert F. Kennedy Jr, causing friction between them and Trump officials. But the Trump administration isn't poised for a complete about-turn on vaccines, says an anonymous former official at the US Department of Health and Human Services. Instead, they’re likely to avoid bringing them up at all during midterm campaigns.

STAT | 7 min read

Features & opinion

The ancient ‘hidden kingdom’ revealed

Fungi decompose quickly — so they’re almost entirely absent from the fossil record. Now, new techniques have allowed scientists to recover fungal DNA and spores from human fossils and materials such as pottery. The findings have shed new light on how fungi might have been a diet staple for ancient humans, as well as used as kindling and in fermenting alcohol.

New Scientist | 10 min read

Infographic of the week
Heatwaves and droughts threaten plant health through a web of host-microbiome-pathogen interactions. Shifts in temperature and moisture can alter the diversity of a plant’s microbiomes, which has knock-on effects on its immunity. A weakened immune system could open the door to infections by soil pathogens, which will drive diseases that could threaten food security. Researchers need to better study how climate extremes could affect these interactions so they’re prepared to monitor and mitigate plant diseases as they emerge, write a group of environmental scientists. (Nature Reviews Microbiology | 52 min read) (Xiong, C. et al./Nat. Rev. Microbiol.)

Quote of the week

“Meningococcal bacteria spread through close contact: kissing, sharing drinks, coughing in crowded spaces. Universities, with their halls of residence, freshers’ weeks and nightclubs, are among the most efficient environments imaginable for transmission.”

The ongoing outbreak of meningitis in Kent, United Kingdom, was only a matter of time, argues public-health researcher Philip Broadbent. There is a vaccine for meningococcal group B disease, the strain causing the outbreak, but current university students wouldn’t have received it as children — it has only been routinely offered to babies since 2015. (The Conversation | 4 min read)

Today I’ve found a new appreciation for the role of moss in solving criminal cases. The growth rate of moss has been used to calculate approximately when a human skeleton had been left in some Pennsylvania woods, and the plant was also a key ‘witness’ in solving a case involving almost 30 grave robberies.

Send your forensic analysis of this newsletter to microbiology-briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Jacob Smith, associate editor, Nature Briefing
With contributions by Flora Graham
